verses.commentary: Generous provision.
False evidence.
Morning dawned. With donkeys. Journey home begins.
When you catch up: Why repaid good with evil? Pursuit begins. Accusation prepared.
The cup for drinking and divination. Joseph poses as Egyptian ruler using Egyptian practices. The accusation heightens the crisis.
Caught up. Repeated these words.
Shocked denial. Innocent protest.
We brought back the silver from Canaan. Why would we now steal? Logic defends them. But logic won't save them.
He will die. Extreme oath. Confident innocence. Rash promise.
Very well. Others go free.
Opened it. No fear.
Steward searched. Systematic search. Suspense builds. The favored son. The one they promised to protect.
All loaded donkeys. Grief and shock. All return together. Critical moment: will they repeat past sin?
Judah leads (mentioned by name). Desperate appeal.
Joseph accuses.
Judah offers no defense. God has uncovered our guilt. He means Joseph, but the words reach deeper: guilt from twenty-two years ago. The brothers finally confess.
Far be it. The test reaches climax: will they leave Benjamin?
Let me speak. Don't be angry. Judah approaches. Humble petition. Deferential tone.
Judah recounts Joseph's questions, showing he paid attention. The speech is masterful: he recreates the history to demonstrate their changed hearts.
His brother dead. Father loves him. Joseph is dead (they think).
I can see for myself. Joseph's demand recounted.
If he leaves, father will die. Prophetic: Jacob would die of grief.
The ultimatum repeated.
Reporting conversation.
The family's need.
We cannot see the man's face unless our youngest brother is with us. Cannot see man unless youngest with us.
Jacob's words. My wife. Rachel. The favored wife.
And I have not seen him since. Haven't seen since. Jacob's conclusion. Permanent loss. How does Joseph feel hearing this?
Harm comes. Jacob's fear. Losing Benjamin would kill him.
Life intertwined. Losing Benjamin means death.
He will die. Certain death. Fatal grief.
Judah's personal pledge (43:9). Honor at stake.
Now then. Substitutionary sacrifice. Total transformation from the man who sold Joseph (37:26-27).
Judah's climax: How can I return without him? Don't let me see my father's misery. The man who sold Joseph now offers himself for Benjamin. Transformation complete.
